book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.

classification.cautionReason: Conciliar or creedal material received in mainstream Nicene Christian tradition, with denominational differences on some canons.

verses.text: It was strictly speaking a court movement, backed by the army, and whenever the images were laid low and their veneration condemned it was by the power of the State, enforcing its will upon a yielding and (as we would call them to-day) Erastian clergy.

verses.text: At that time the only Patriarch who sided with the heretics was John the Grammarian, the Patriarch of Constantinople, the very same who in 814 had repudiated the iconoclast doctrine!

verses.text: With the death of this Emperor, the power of the Iconoclasts likewise died; and at the accession of Michael III with his mother Theodora and his sister Thecla came the final triumph of the images.

verses.text: This Synod decreed that a Feast of Orthodoxy (ἡ κυριακὴ τῆς ὀρθοδοξίας ) should be celebrated annually, at which the victory over the iconoclasts should be regularly remembered.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: Much has been written, and truly written, of the superiority of the iconoclastic rulers; but when all has been said that can be, the fact still remains, that they were most of them but sorry Christians, and the justice of the Protestant Archbishop of Dublin’s summing up of the matter will not be disputed by any impartial student.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: Had the Iconoclasts triumphed, when their work showed itself at last in its true colours, it would have proved to be the triumph, not of faith in an invisible God, but of frivolous unbelief in an incarnate Saviour.”

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: We come now to consider what reception the Seventh of the General Councils met with in the West.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: And first we find that it was accepted, so far at least as its dogmatic decrees went, by the Pope, the whole Roman Church and, so far as we know, by all the West except the realm of Charlemagne and, as would naturally be expected, the English Church.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: It is true that this was a large and very important exception; so large and so important that it becomes necessary to examine in detail the causes which led to this rejection.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: Some persons have supposed that the English council held at Calcuth in 787 rejected the ecumenical character of II.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: Nice, because in two of its canons (the 1st and the 4th) it only speaks of “the faith of the Six General Councils.”

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: But it is evident that the reason for this was that it had not yet heard of the Nicene synod; moreover such action would have been clearly impossible, since the council was presided over by the Bishop of Ostia, the legate of Pope Hadrian.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: The first opposition to the council in the West was made apparently by Charlemagne himself.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: Pope Hadrian sent him a translation of the acts into Latin and signified his acceptance of the council.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: But this translation was so badly done that not only was a large part of the acts utterly unintelligible, but also, in at least one place, a bishop of the council was made to say that the sacred images were to be adored with the same supreme worship as is paid to the Holy Trinity.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: It may not be wholly charitable to suggest the possibility of such a thing having any influence in the matter.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: On the other hand it would be unfair to the reader not to state that Charlemagne had, or thought that he had, serious grievances against the Empress Irene, and that he might not have been sorry to have discovered some reason for which to reject her council.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: It should, moreover, be remembered how much the Pope in his struggle for independence of the Eastern Empire trusted to Charlemagne, and therefore how reluctant he might readily have been to break with so important an ally; and so might be induced to tolerate the rejection by the Frankish Emperor of what had been received by him, the Vicar of Christ and the successor of Peter, as the Seventh Ecumenical Synod of the Catholic Church.

verses.book: The Seventh Ecumenical Council. The Second Council of Nice.
verses.text: As a result of this feeling of Charlemagne’s, there were written what we call the “Caroline Books,” and these exercised so mighty an influence on this whole question, and so completely misled even the learned, that I shall give a careful examination of their authorship, authority, and contents; for there can be no doubt that it was the influence of these books (which appeared in 790) that induced the unfortunate action of the Council of Frankfort four years later (in 794); and that of the Convention of Paris in 825.
